meta.title: Curated demonstration data for the Military History Elo engine
meta.description: A deliberately small, hand-curated seed spanning selected conflicts from the Late Bronze Age through 2021. It exists to exercise entity resets, tactical/operational/strategic layers, coalition participation, participant-specific stakes, and differentiated defeat types. It is not a ranking-ready census of all countries, empires, wars, campaigns, or battles.

year_range.calendar_note: Negative integers denote BCE years using the project's integer-year convention; no claim of day-level precision is made.

layer_policy.double_counting_note: Nested events update different rating tracks. A battle, campaign, and final war settlement must not all update one undifferentiated Elo stream.

continuity_policy.default: A polity conventionally treated as a new country, empire, or regime-state receives a new entity ID and begins from the model baseline. Predecessor links are descriptive only.
continuity_policy.roman_byzantine_convention: For this project, the Roman Empire rating ends at 395 and the Byzantine Empire begins at 395 with a complete Elo reset. This is an explicit project convention, not a claim that scholarship supplies one uncontested Byzantine start date.

outcome_policy.stakes_and_scale: Participant stakes and national_scale are coded separately from event-wide scope and scale.

termination_examples.united_states_vietnam: withdrawal_after_failed_intervention; major strategic defeat with homeland sovereignty preserved

source_policy.purpose: Stable academic, official-history, museum, structured-data, and edited general-reference links support this demonstration.
source_policy.provenance_limit: An event-level URL is not a substitute for claim-level citations, page references, source-family deduplication, or multilingual scholarly review in production data.
source_policy.licensing: Only original normalized seed assertions are stored. Linked copyrighted references are cited rather than copied. Government-work and open-data license labels should still be checked at item level before redistribution.

known_limitations: Selection is illustrative and strongly incomplete; absence of an entity or event is not evidence of military inactivity or failure.
Several coalition records intentionally rate a documented dyadic or principal-participant slice because the seed roster does not yet contain every ally, vassal, client, or non-state actor.
Ancient dates, force sizes, casualties, and even outcomes can be highly uncertain; this seed usually omits force_size rather than encode false precision.
Cross-era raw Elo values are not directly identifiable because opponent networks are disconnected and military technology changes radically.
The preferred cross-era display is contemporary-network percentile with uncertainty, not a literal claim that two entities separated by centuries could be compared as if they fought.

The source list is suitable for a demonstration build, not yet for the project's stated goal of maximum historical accuracy. Production ingestion requires claim-level provenance and expert adjudication.
recommended_use: Human review of taxonomy and continuity rules
meta.prohibited_interpretation: Do not publish this seed's leaderboard as a definitive ranking of the most militarily successful states or empires in history.
meta.comparison_warning: Ratings in disconnected opponent networks are not directly identifiable; the historical-success index uses within-network percentiles.

entities.name: New Kingdom of Egypt
entities.continuity_note: Curated demonstration boundary; dynastic precision is intentionally not implied.

entities.name: Hittite Empire
entities.continuity_note: Broad conventional imperial period used for demonstration.

entities.name: Achaemenid Empire
entities.continuity_note: Ends as a rated entity with the Macedonian conquest; successor regimes do not inherit its rating.

entities.name: Athens
entities.continuity_note: Classical democratic and imperial-era Athens only.

entities.name: Sparta
entities.continuity_note: Conventional independent Spartan polity period for this demonstration.

entities.name: Macedonian Empire of Alexander
entities.continuity_note: Alexander's imperial polity; the Diadochi successor kingdoms require separate future entities.

entities.name: Carthage
entities.continuity_note: Ends with the destruction of Carthage in the Third Punic War.

entities.name: Roman Republic
entities.continuity_note: Separate rated polity from the Roman Empire under the project's historiographic-entity rule.

entities.name: Roman Empire
entities.region: Europe, North Africa, and West Asia
entities.continuity_note: The project ends this rating line at the permanent 395 division. The Byzantine rating starts independently at baseline.

entities.name: Byzantine Empire
entities.continuity_note: Explicit project convention: new entity and full Elo reset at 395. The predecessor link is descriptive and transfers no rating.

entities.name: Sasanian Empire
entities.continuity_note: Ends as a polity after the Rashidun conquest; later Iranian states receive new IDs.

entities.name: Rashidun Caliphate
entities.continuity_note: Rated separately from the subsequent Umayyad Caliphate.

entities.name: Umayyad Caliphate
entities.region: West Asia, North Africa, and Iberia
entities.continuity_note: New dynastic imperial entity and independent rating line.

entities.name: Frankish Kingdom
entities.continuity_note: Broad Frankish polity used for the Tours demonstration record; later partitions are not merged into this rating.

entities.name: Mongol Empire
entities.continuity_note: Unified imperial line only; khanates require separate future records.

entities.name: Mamluk Sultanate
entities.continuity_note: Cairo-based Mamluk Sultanate through Ottoman conquest.

entities.name: Ottoman Empire
entities.region: Southeastern Europe, West Asia, and North Africa
entities.continuity_note: The Republic of Turkey would be a new entity and does not inherit this rating.

entities.name: Safavid Iran
entities.continuity_note: Distinct Safavid polity; later Iranian dynasties require separate ratings.

entities.name: Mughal Empire
entities.continuity_note: Conventional Mughal imperial span; periods of reduced control remain part of the same demonstration entity.

entities.name: Spanish Monarchy and Empire
entities.continuity_note: Aggregated imperial Spanish polity for demonstration; future production data should adjudicate regime boundaries more finely.

entities.name: Dutch Revolt and Republic
entities.continuity_note: Begins with the revolt for this demonstration so its independence war can be rated; the later Kingdom of the Netherlands is separate.

entities.name: Kingdom of France
entities.continuity_note: Ends before revolutionary and Napoleonic entities, which receive independent ratings.

entities.name: First French Empire
entities.continuity_note: Explicit new imperial entity; predecessor link is lineage metadata and transfers no Elo.

entities.name: United Kingdom
entities.region: Western Europe and former overseas empire
entities.continuity_note: The sovereign United Kingdom is the rated entity; imperial status is an era tag rather than a successor ID here.

entities.name: Russian Empire
entities.continuity_note: Ends in 1917; the Soviet Union is independently initialized.

entities.name: German Empire
entities.continuity_note: Ends with the imperial regime in 1918; Nazi Germany is a later, independently rated entity.

entities.name: Nazi Germany
entities.continuity_note: Treated as a distinct historiographic regime-state. It ends in terminal military defeat, occupation, and regime destruction in 1945.

entities.name: Empire of Japan
entities.continuity_note: Imperial constitutional and expansionist-era entity; postwar Japan requires a new record for future conflicts.

entities.name: Soviet Union
entities.continuity_note: New polity and baseline reset; no Russian Empire Elo is inherited.

entities.name: United States
entities.continuity_note: Continuous rated polity from the declaration-era independence struggle for this demonstration.

entities.name: Democratic Republic of Vietnam
entities.continuity_note: Distinct wartime state; unified post-1976 Vietnam would be a successor entity in expanded data.

entities.name: Republic of Vietnam
entities.continuity_note: Ends in existential defeat and state dissolution in 1975.

entities.name: People's Republic of China
entities.continuity_note: Begins as a new polity in 1949; predecessor civil-war actors are outside this seed.

entities.name: Islamic Republic of Afghanistan
entities.continuity_note: Rated only for its 2004-2021 existence; it ends in state collapse during the Taliban offensive.

entities.name: Taliban
entities.continuity_note: Included because state-versus-insurgent wars cannot be rated accurately without the opposing armed actor; governmental episodes are not treated as inherited from the Afghan Republic.

events.name: Battle of Kadesh
events.summary: A heavily propagandized battle generally treated as tactically inconclusive; the Hittites retained Kadesh, so mission and positional scores modestly favor them.

events.name: Battle of Salamis
events.summary: A major Greek naval victory and turning point; exact fleet strengths and tactical details remain disputed.

events.name: Greco-Persian Wars
events.summary: The Greek coalition prevented Achaemenid conquest of mainland Greece and later secured an advantageous settlement; Persia remained a powerful sovereign empire.

events.name: Peloponnesian War
events.summary: Sparta defeated Athens, dismantled the Athenian empire, and imposed a settlement, while Athens continued to exist as a polity.

events.name: Battle of Gaugamela
events.summary: Alexander won a decisive field victory over Darius III; ancient force totals are too uncertain for this seed to encode them.

events.name: Macedonian Conquest of the Achaemenid Empire
events.summary: Alexander's campaigns destroyed Achaemenid imperial sovereignty and transferred its principal territories to the Macedonian conqueror.

events.name: Battle of Cannae
events.summary: Hannibal's army destroyed a much larger Roman field force, a decisive Carthaginian tactical victory that did not by itself decide the war.

events.name: Battle of Zama
events.summary: Scipio's victory over Hannibal ended major resistance and led directly to the Carthaginian peace settlement.

events.name: Second Punic War
events.summary: Rome survived severe battlefield defeats, ultimately defeated Carthage, and imposed a settlement that sharply reduced Carthaginian power without yet ending the state.

events.name: Third Punic War
events.summary: Rome destroyed Carthage as an independent polity; this is coded as terminal conquest rather than merely another loss.

events.name: Battle of Edessa
events.summary: Shapur I defeated and captured the Roman emperor Valerian; the Sasanian victory was decisive although it did not end Roman sovereignty.

events.name: Julian's Persian Campaign
events.summary: The Roman invasion failed after Julian's death; Jovian withdrew and ceded strategic positions in the resulting peace.

events.name: Byzantine-Sasanian War of 602-628
events.summary: Heraclius reversed extensive Sasanian conquests and restored the prewar frontier, but both empires were badly exhausted and the settlement proved short-lived.

events.name: Battle of Yarmouk
events.summary: A decisive Rashidun victory that broke the principal Byzantine field response in Syria and enabled durable regional conquest.

events.name: Rashidun Conquest of the Sasanian Empire
events.summary: Rashidun forces conquered the Sasanian realm, ending Sasanian imperial sovereignty in 651.

events.name: Umayyad Siege of Constantinople
events.summary: The Byzantine defense defeated a major Umayyad land-and-sea siege; Bulgaria's important contribution is acknowledged but not separately rated in this limited seed.

events.name: Battle of Tours
events.summary: A Frankish battlefield victory that ended this Umayyad expedition; the seed avoids the older claim that one battle alone determined the future of Europe.

events.name: Battle of Ain Jalut
events.summary: A clear Mamluk victory over a Mongol field force that checked the immediate advance into Egypt; it is not coded as the destruction of the wider Mongol Empire.

events.name: Siege and Fall of Constantinople
events.summary: The operational siege record is separate from the strategic state-extinction record, preventing tactical and strategic results from being conflated within one rating track.

events.name: Final Ottoman-Byzantine War
events.summary: The Ottoman conquest of Constantinople ended the Byzantine Empire as a sovereign polity; its Elo line terminates rather than transferring to the victor.

events.name: Battle of Chaldiran
events.summary: A decisive Ottoman battlefield victory over the Safavids, aided by firearms and prepared defenses; it did not destroy Safavid sovereignty.

events.name: Eighty Years' War
events.summary: The Dutch revolt secured durable independence recognized in 1648; Spain suffered a major anti-colonial loss but remained sovereign and retained other possessions.

events.name: Mughal-Safavid War over Kandahar
events.summary: Safavid Iran captured and retained Kandahar despite repeated Mughal recovery attempts; both empires remained sovereign.

sources.title: Kandahar: From the Mongol invasion through the Safavid era

events.name: Seven Years' War
events.summary: This seed records the British-French strategic dyad within a much larger coalition war; Britain made major imperial gains while France remained a sovereign great power.

events.name: American Revolutionary War
events.summary: The United States secured recognized independence. Britain lost the colonies but neither its homeland sovereignty nor imperial state, distinguishing this from terminal defeat.

events.name: French Invasion of Russia
events.summary: Napoleon reached and occupied Moscow but failed to compel Russia and lost most of the invading army during retreat; this is an operational Russian victory despite battlefield ambiguity at individual engagements.

sources.title: Napoleonic Wars: The retreat from Moscow

events.name: Napoleonic Wars
events.summary: Coalition victory ended the First French Empire and restored the Bourbon monarchy; the French nation survived, but this rated imperial entity ceased.

events.name: Crimean War
events.summary: The allied coalition compelled Russia to accept an unfavorable peace and checked its immediate objectives; no participant state was destroyed.

events.name: Russo-Japanese War
events.summary: Japan won major land and naval victories and secured a favorable settlement; Russia's regime and core sovereignty survived.

events.name: Battle of Tannenberg
events.summary: German forces encircled and destroyed much of the Russian Second Army, a decisive tactical victory within a war Germany ultimately lost.

events.name: First World War
events.summary: The German Empire was defeated, lost territory, and ceased as an imperial regime. This demonstration records Britain and the late-entering United States but does not pretend to represent the entire Allied coalition.

events.name: Battle of Midway
events.summary: A decisive U.S. carrier victory that halted the Japanese attempt to seize Midway and changed the naval balance, without by itself ending the war.

events.name: German-Soviet War and Eastern Front Campaign
events.summary: Germany failed to defeat the Soviet Union and was driven back to Berlin; this operational record is separate from the final strategic surrender record.

events.name: Pacific War
events.summary: Imperial Japan surrendered unconditionally, lost its conquests, was occupied, and its prewar imperial political-military order ended; the Japanese nation itself continued.

events.name: Second World War in Europe
events.summary: Nazi Germany suffered unconditional military defeat, occupation, regime destruction, and territorial loss. It is therefore a terminal defeat, unlike an expeditionary withdrawal such as the United States in Vietnam.

events.name: Chosin Reservoir Campaign
events.summary: China forced a UN withdrawal from northeastern Korea, while U.S.-led forces preserved much of their combat power and completed an organized evacuation; the multidimensional record is intentionally mixed.

events.name: Tet Offensive
events.summary: U.S. and South Vietnamese forces defeated the attacks militarily and inflicted heavy losses, while Tet had wider political-strategic effects represented by the separate war layer rather than reversing this operational coding.

sources.title: The Paris Peace Accords and the end of the Vietnam War, 1969-1975

events.name: Vietnam War
events.summary: Participant-specific outcomes prevent false equivalence: the United States failed its intervention and withdrew without losing homeland sovereignty; South Vietnam ceased to exist; North Vietnam achieved decisive reunification.

sources.title: The Paris Peace Accords and the end of the Vietnam War, 1969-1975

events.name: Taliban Offensive of 2021
events.summary: The Taliban rapidly seized national control and Kabul as Afghan state forces disintegrated; the United States evacuated while acting as a withdrawing supporting participant.

sources.title: What We Need to Learn: Lessons from Twenty Years of Afghanistan Reconstruction

events.name: War in Afghanistan
events.summary: The United States withdrew after failing to create a durable outcome, the Afghan Republic collapsed, and the Taliban returned to national power; participant consequences are scored separately.

sources.title: What We Need to Learn: Lessons from Twenty Years of Afghanistan Reconstruction
